data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse resulted from a climate-induced famine that triggered a migratory domino effect, where displaced groups consumed the resources of neighboring states.", "mechanism": "A prolonged megadrought (verified by pollen cores) caused crop failures across the Eastern Mediterranean. This forced mass migration of starving populations (the 'Sea Peoples') who moved sequentially from west to east or north to south. Each arriving group stripped the next polity of its remaining grain reserves, creating a chain reaction of state failure that Egypt halted only through massive military expenditure and geographic luck.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states", "Cyprus"], "excludes": ["Nubia", "Assyria"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoclimatic data indicates normal rainfall levels in the Aegean and Anatolia between 1200-1150 BC.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "An atypical emphasis on the 'domino' mechanic of migration rather than just the drought itself; often debated regarding the directionality of the movement."}
